            <paragraph>32-year-old female presented to ED via EMS at 15:30 with acute onset allergic reaction. Patient reports eating shrimp scampi at local restaurant approximately 20 minutes prior to symptom onset. Within 5-10 minutes of eating, developed generalized urticaria, throat tightness, and difficulty breathing. Friend called 911 immediately.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>EMS arrived within 8 minutes and found patient in moderate distress with audible wheezing, tachypneic, diffuse hives, and facial angioedema. Patient was alert and oriented but anxious. EMS administered Epinephrine 0.3mg IM to right thigh at 15:20 with some improvement in breathing. IV access obtained en route. Patient transported to ED with lights and sirens, arrival at 15:30.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Patient denies prior history of shellfish allergy. Reports eating shrimp "many times before" without issue. Last ate shrimp approximately 6 months ago without problems. Denies recent illness, new medications, or other new exposures. Takes no daily medications. No history of asthma or prior anaphylaxis.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Review of systems: Denies chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. Endorses severe anxiety and feeling of "impending doom." Denies loss of consciousness or confusion.</paragraph>
          </text>

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">15:30 - ED Arrival and Initial Assessment:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient arrived via EMS with acute anaphylaxis following shellfish ingestion. EMS had already administered first dose of Epinephrine IM (0.3mg) at 15:20 with partial response. Upon arrival, patient in moderate respiratory distress with persistent wheezing, urticaria, and facial angioedema. Initial vital signs concerning for continued anaphylaxis with tachycardia (HR 112), tachypnea (RR 26), and borderline hypoxia (SpO2 93% on RA).</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">15:35-15:50 - Acute Management:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient immediately placed on continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry. IV access already established by EMS. Given persistent symptoms despite EMS epinephrine, SECOND dose of Epinephrine 0.3mg IM administered to left thigh at 15:35. Patient placed on 4L O2 via nasal cannula with improvement in SpO2 to 96%. Administered IV Diphenhydramine 50mg, IV Famotidine 20mg. Normal saline bolus initiated for volume resuscitation given relative hypotension.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">16:00-16:30 - Continued Treatment:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient showing gradual improvement but continued to have significant wheezing. Labs sent including serum tryptase (drawn at 1 hour post-symptom onset). Chest x-ray obtained to rule out aspiration or other complications - negative. ECG showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. Repeat albuterol treatment at 16:30 resolved remaining bronchospasm.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">16:30-18:00 - Observation Period:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient observed for biphasic reaction (can occur 4-12 hours after initial reaction in 20% of cases). Vital signs stabilized with normalization of HR and RR. Oxygen weaned to 2L then room air with maintained SpO2 99%. Urticaria and angioedema markedly improved. Patient able to ambulate without distress. Serum tryptase returned elevated at 18.5 mcg/L, confirming anaphylaxis diagnosis. Patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout 3-hour observation period with no recurrence of symptoms.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">18:00-18:30 - Discharge Planning:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>After minimum 3-hour observation period with complete resolution of respiratory symptoms and stable vital signs, patient deemed safe for discharge. Extensive education provided regarding shellfish allergy, avoidance strategies, and recognition of anaphylaxis symptoms. Patient understands need to avoid ALL shellfish and crustaceans. Prescribed EpiPen auto-injector with detailed training on proper use. Instructed to return immediately if any symptoms recur. Allergist referral placed for comprehensive allergy testing and long-term management. Patient verbalizes understanding of all discharge instructions and demonstrates proper EpiPen technique.</paragraph>
          </text>

            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Immediate Actions:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Complete 3-day course of prednisone to prevent biphasic reaction</item>
              <item>ALWAYS carry two EpiPen auto-injectors</item>
              <item>Complete avoidance of all shellfish and crustaceans</item>
              <item>Obtain medical alert bracelet identifying shellfish allergy</item>
              <item>Educate family/friends about allergy and EpiPen use</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Follow-up Care:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Allergist appointment within 2 weeks (URGENT)
                <list>
                  <item>Comprehensive allergy evaluation</item>
                  <item>Skin prick testing or specific IgE testing</item>
                  <item>Evaluate cross-reactivity with other seafood (fish, mollusks)</item>
                  <item>Consider testing for other potential food allergies</item>
                  <item>Provide written anaphylaxis action plan</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>Primary care physician within 1 week
                <list>
                  <item>Review ED course and medications</item>
                  <item>Ensure EpiPen prescription filled</item>
                  <item>Update problem list and allergy list</item>
                  <item>Coordinate with allergist</item>
                </list>
              </item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Long-term Management:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Lifelong shellfish avoidance</item>
              <item>Always carry epinephrine auto-injectors (replace before expiration)</item>
              <item>Wear medical identification jewelry</item>
              <item>Inform all healthcare providers of severe allergy</item>
              <item>Educate about cross-contamination risks in restaurants</item>
              <item>Annual allergist follow-up recommended</item>
              <item>Consider food allergy action plan for workplace/school</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Biphasic Reaction Monitoring:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Remain under observation by responsible adult for 24 hours</item>
              <item>Peak risk for biphasic reaction is 4-12 hours post-initial reaction</item>
              <item>Keep EpiPen immediately accessible</item>

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">15:30 - ED Arrival and Initial Assessment:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient arrived via EMS with acute anaphylaxis following shellfish ingestion. EMS had already administered first dose of Epinephrine IM (0.3mg) at 15:20 with partial response. Upon arrival, patient in moderate respiratory distress with persistent wheezing, urticaria, and facial angioedema. Initial vital signs concerning for continued anaphylaxis with tachycardia (HR 112), tachypnea (RR 26), and borderline hypoxia (SpO2 93% on RA).</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">15:35-15:50 - Acute Management:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient immediately placed on continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry. IV access already established by EMS. Given persistent symptoms despite EMS epinephrine, SECOND dose of Epinephrine 0.3mg IM administered to left thigh at 15:35. Patient placed on 4L O2 via nasal cannula with improvement in SpO2 to 96%. Administered IV Diphenhydramine 50mg, IV Famotidine 20mg. Normal saline bolus initiated for volume resuscitation given relative hypotension.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">16:00-16:30 - Continued Treatment:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient showing gradual improvement but continued to have significant wheezing. Labs sent including serum tryptase (drawn at 1 hour post-symptom onset). Chest x-ray obtained to rule out aspiration or other complications - negative. ECG showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. Repeat albuterol treatment at 16:30 resolved remaining bronchospasm.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">16:30-18:00 - Observation Period:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient observed for biphasic reaction (can occur 4-12 hours after initial reaction in 20% of cases). Vital signs stabilized with normalization of HR and RR. Oxygen weaned to 2L then room air with maintained SpO2 99%. Urticaria and angioedema markedly improved. Patient able to ambulate without distress. Serum tryptase returned elevated at 18.5 mcg/L, confirming anaphylaxis diagnosis. Patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout 3-hour observation period with no recurrence of symptoms.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">18:00-18:30 - Discharge Planning:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>After minimum 3-hour observation period with complete resolution of respiratory symptoms and stable vital signs, patient deemed safe for discharge. Extensive education provided regarding shellfish allergy, avoidance strategies, and recognition of anaphylaxis symptoms. Patient understands need to avoid ALL shellfish and crustaceans. Prescribed EpiPen auto-injector with detailed training on proper use. Instructed to return immediately if any symptoms recur. Allergist referral placed for comprehensive allergy testing and long-term management. Patient verbalizes understanding of all discharge instructions and demonstrates proper EpiPen technique.</paragraph>
          </text>

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Immediate Actions:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Complete 3-day course of prednisone to prevent biphasic reaction</item>
              <item>ALWAYS carry two EpiPen auto-injectors</item>
              <item>Complete avoidance of all shellfish and crustaceans</item>
              <item>Obtain medical alert bracelet identifying shellfish allergy</item>
              <item>Educate family/friends about allergy and EpiPen use</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Follow-up Care:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Allergist appointment within 2 weeks (URGENT)
                <list>
                  <item>Comprehensive allergy evaluation</item>
                  <item>Skin prick testing or specific IgE testing</item>
                  <item>Evaluate cross-reactivity with other seafood (fish, mollusks)</item>
                  <item>Consider testing for other potential food allergies</item>
                  <item>Provide written anaphylaxis action plan</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>Primary care physician within 1 week
                <list>
                  <item>Review ED course and medications</item>
                  <item>Ensure EpiPen prescription filled</item>
                  <item>Update problem list and allergy list</item>
                  <item>Coordinate with allergist</item>
                </list>
              </item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Long-term Management:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Lifelong shellfish avoidance</item>
              <item>Always carry epinephrine auto-injectors (replace before expiration)</item>
              <item>Wear medical identification jewelry</item>
              <item>Inform all healthcare providers of severe allergy</item>
              <item>Educate about cross-contamination risks in restaurants</item>
              <item>Annual allergist follow-up recommended</item>
              <item>Consider food allergy action plan for workplace/school</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Biphasic Reaction Monitoring:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Remain under observation by responsible adult for 24 hours</item>
              <item>Peak risk for biphasic reaction is 4-12 hours post-initial reaction</item>
              <item>Keep EpiPen immediately accessible</item>
              <item>Use EpiPen and call 911 if ANY symptoms recur</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Patient Education Completed:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Recognition of anaphylaxis symptoms</item>
              <item>EpiPen administration technique (demonstrated correctly)</item>
              <item>Importance of calling 911 after EpiPen use</item>
              <item>Shellfish avoidance strategies</item>
              <item>Cross-contamination awareness</item>
              <item>Biphasic reaction risk and monitoring</item>
              <item>Return precautions</item>
              <item>Written discharge instructions provided</item>
            </list>
          </text>

Medication Dose Route Frequency Duration Instructions
Epinephrine Auto-Injector (EpiPen) 0.3 mg IM (intramuscular) As needed for allergic reaction CARRY AT ALL TIMES CRITICAL: Use immediately if experiencing difficulty breathing, throat tightness, or widespread hives. Inject into outer thigh. Call 911 immediately after use. ALWAYS CARRY TWO EpiPens. Check expiration date monthly.
Prednisone 60 mg Oral Once daily 3 days Take in morning with food. Prevents late-phase allergic reaction. Complete full course even if feeling better. May cause increased appetite, difficulty sleeping, mood changes.
Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg Oral Every 6 hours as needed 48 hours For residual itching or hives. May cause drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery. Keep on hand for minor allergic reactions (but use EpiPen for severe symptoms).
Famotidine (Pepcid) 20 mg Oral Twice daily 3 days H2-blocker to reduce allergic inflammation. Take morning and evening.
MEDICATION SAFETY: EpiPen is life-saving medication - do NOT wait to use if experiencing severe allergic symptoms After using EpiPen, you MUST go to emergency department even if feeling better Store EpiPen at room temperature, avoid extreme heat or cold Check liquid in EpiPen window - should be clear, not discolored Get refills before expiration date Inform family, friends, coworkers about allergy and how to help if needed

            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">DIAGNOSIS: Anaphylaxis (Severe Allergic Reaction) to Shellfish</content></paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">WHAT HAPPENED:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>You had a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction called anaphylaxis after eating shrimp. This caused difficulty breathing, hives all over your body, and facial swelling. You were treated with two doses of epinephrine (adrenaline), antihistamines, steroids, and breathing treatments. You responded well to treatment.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">ALLERGY AVOIDANCE - CRITICAL:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">NEVER eat shellfish or crustaceans again</content> - including shrimp, lobster, crab, crayfish, prawns</item>
              <item>Read ALL food labels carefully - shellfish can be hidden in soups, sauces, Asian dishes, seafood restaurants</item>
              <item>Be cautious at seafood restaurants - high risk of cross-contamination even with non-shellfish dishes</item>
              <item>Inform all restaurants about your SEVERE shellfish allergy</item>
              <item>Consider wearing medical alert bracelet</item>
              <item>Inform all healthcare providers about this allergy</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">YOUR EPIPEN - LIFE-SAVING INFORMATION:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">ALWAYS carry TWO EpiPens with you at all times</content></item>
              <item>Use EpiPen immediately if you experience ANY of these symptoms:
                <list>
                  <item>Difficulty breathing or throat tightness</item>
                  <item>Widespread hives or rapid swelling</item>
                  <item>Dizziness or feeling faint</item>
                  <item>Sense that "something is very wrong"</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>HOW TO USE EPIPEN:
                <list>
                  <item>Remove from carrier tube</item>
                  <item>Hold firmly with orange tip pointing down</item>
                  <item>Remove blue safety cap by pulling straight up</item>
                  <item>Place orange tip against outer thigh (can go through clothing)</item>
                  <item>Push down hard until you hear a click</item>
                  <item>Hold in place for 3 seconds</item>
                  <item>Remove and massage injection site</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">After using EpiPen: ALWAYS call 911 and go to emergency department</content></item>
              <item>You may need second dose if symptoms don't improve in 5-10 minutes</item>
              <item>Store at room temperature (not in car or refrigerator)</item>
              <item>Check expiration date monthly and get refills</item>
              <item>Teach family/friends how to use EpiPen</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">MEDICATIONS:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Take prednisone 60mg daily for 3 days (prevents late reaction)</item>
              <item>Take famotidine 20mg twice daily for 3 days</item>
              <item>Take Benadryl 50mg as needed for residual itching</item>
              <item>Complete all medications even if feeling better</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">WARNING - BIPHASIC REACTION:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>In 20% of anaphylaxis cases, symptoms can return 4-12 hours later even after initial improvement. This is called a biphasic reaction. For this reason:</paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Have someone stay with you tonight</item>
              <item>Keep EpiPen within arm's reach at all times</item>
              <item>Avoid alcohol and strenuous activity for 24 hours</item>
              <item>Do not hesitate to use EpiPen and call 911 if ANY symptoms return</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">RETURN TO EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT IMMEDIATELY IF:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Difficulty breathing or swallowing</item>
              <item>Chest tightness or throat closing sensation</item>
              <item>New or worsening hives</item>
              <item>Facial, lip, or tongue swelling</item>
              <item>Dizziness, lightheadedness, or feeling faint</item>
              <item>Rapid heartbeat or irregular heartbeat</item>
              <item>Severe abdominal cramping or vomiting</item>
              <item>Confusion or feeling of impending doom</item>
              <item>ANY concern that allergic reaction is returning</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENTS:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">Allergist appointment within 2 weeks</content> (referral placed - they will call you)
                <list>
                  <item>Comprehensive allergy testing</item>
                  <item>Evaluate for other potential food allergies</item>
                  <item>Discuss cross-reactivity with other seafood</item>
                  <item>Long-term management plan</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>Primary care doctor within 1 week for follow-up</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">ACTIVITY:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Rest today and tomorrow</item>
              <item>Avoid strenuous exercise for 24-48 hours</item>
              <item>Benadryl may cause drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery</item>
              <item>Can return to normal activities once feeling completely better</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">QUESTIONS ANSWERED:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient verbalizes understanding of:</paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Need to avoid all shellfish permanently</item>
              <item>How and when to use EpiPen</item>
              <item>Importance of carrying two EpiPens at all times</item>
              <item>Warning signs requiring immediate return to ED</item>
              <item>Risk of biphasic reaction in next 12 hours</item>
              <item>Need for allergist follow-up</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Patient received written discharge instructions and demonstrates proper EpiPen technique.</content></paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Discharge time: 18:30</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Discharge condition: Stable, symptoms resolved</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Discharged to: Home with responsible adult</paragraph>
          </text>

Medication Dose Route Frequency Duration Instructions
Epinephrine Auto-Injector (EpiPen) 0.3 mg IM (intramuscular) As needed for allergic reaction CARRY AT ALL TIMES CRITICAL: Use immediately if experiencing difficulty breathing, throat tightness, or widespread hives. Inject into outer thigh. Call 911 immediately after use. ALWAYS CARRY TWO EpiPens. Check expiration date monthly.
Prednisone 60 mg Oral Once daily 3 days Take in morning with food. Prevents late-phase allergic reaction. Complete full course even if feeling better. May cause increased appetite, difficulty sleeping, mood changes.
Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg Oral Every 6 hours as needed 48 hours For residual itching or hives. May cause drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery. Keep on hand for minor allergic reactions (but use EpiPen for severe symptoms).
Famotidine (Pepcid) 20 mg Oral Twice daily 3 days H2-blocker to reduce allergic inflammation. Take morning and evening.
MEDICATION SAFETY: EpiPen is life-saving medication - do NOT wait to use if experiencing severe allergic symptoms After using EpiPen, you MUST go to emergency department even if feeling better Store EpiPen at room temperature, avoid extreme heat or cold Check liquid in EpiPen window - should be clear, not discolored Get refills before expiration date Inform family, friends, coworkers about allergy and how to help if needed

            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">DIAGNOSIS: Anaphylaxis (Severe Allergic Reaction) to Shellfish</content></paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">WHAT HAPPENED:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>You had a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction called anaphylaxis after eating shrimp. This caused difficulty breathing, hives all over your body, and facial swelling. You were treated with two doses of epinephrine (adrenaline), antihistamines, steroids, and breathing treatments. You responded well to treatment.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">ALLERGY AVOIDANCE - CRITICAL:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">NEVER eat shellfish or crustaceans again</content> - including shrimp, lobster, crab, crayfish, prawns</item>
              <item>Read ALL food labels carefully - shellfish can be hidden in soups, sauces, Asian dishes, seafood restaurants</item>
              <item>Be cautious at seafood restaurants - high risk of cross-contamination even with non-shellfish dishes</item>
              <item>Inform all restaurants about your SEVERE shellfish allergy</item>
              <item>Consider wearing medical alert bracelet</item>
              <item>Inform all healthcare providers about this allergy</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">YOUR EPIPEN - LIFE-SAVING INFORMATION:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">ALWAYS carry TWO EpiPens with you at all times</content></item>
              <item>Use EpiPen immediately if you experience ANY of these symptoms:
                <list>
                  <item>Difficulty breathing or throat tightness</item>
                  <item>Widespread hives or rapid swelling</item>
                  <item>Dizziness or feeling faint</item>
                  <item>Sense that "something is very wrong"</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>HOW TO USE EPIPEN:
                <list>
                  <item>Remove from carrier tube</item>
                  <item>Hold firmly with orange tip pointing down</item>
                  <item>Remove blue safety cap by pulling straight up</item>
                  <item>Place orange tip against outer thigh (can go through clothing)</item>
                  <item>Push down hard until you hear a click</item>
                  <item>Hold in place for 3 seconds</item>
                  <item>Remove and massage injection site</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">After using EpiPen: ALWAYS call 911 and go to emergency department</content></item>
              <item>You may need second dose if symptoms don't improve in 5-10 minutes</item>
              <item>Store at room temperature (not in car or refrigerator)</item>
              <item>Check expiration date monthly and get refills</item>
              <item>Teach family/friends how to use EpiPen</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">MEDICATIONS:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Take prednisone 60mg daily for 3 days (prevents late reaction)</item>
              <item>Take famotidine 20mg twice daily for 3 days</item>
              <item>Take Benadryl 50mg as needed for residual itching</item>
              <item>Complete all medications even if feeling better</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">WARNING - BIPHASIC REACTION:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>In 20% of anaphylaxis cases, symptoms can return 4-12 hours later even after initial improvement. This is called a biphasic reaction. For this reason:</paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Have someone stay with you tonight</item>
              <item>Keep EpiPen within arm's reach at all times</item>
              <item>Avoid alcohol and strenuous activity for 24 hours</item>
              <item>Do not hesitate to use EpiPen and call 911 if ANY symptoms return</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">RETURN TO EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT IMMEDIATELY IF:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Difficulty breathing or swallowing</item>
              <item>Chest tightness or throat closing sensation</item>
              <item>New or worsening hives</item>
              <item>Facial, lip, or tongue swelling</item>
              <item>Dizziness, lightheadedness, or feeling faint</item>
              <item>Rapid heartbeat or irregular heartbeat</item>
              <item>Severe abdominal cramping or vomiting</item>
              <item>Confusion or feeling of impending doom</item>
              <item>ANY concern that allergic reaction is returning</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENTS:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">Allergist appointment within 2 weeks</content> (referral placed - they will call you)
                <list>
                  <item>Comprehensive allergy testing</item>
                  <item>Evaluate for other potential food allergies</item>
                  <item>Discuss cross-reactivity with other seafood</item>
                  <item>Long-term management plan</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>Primary care doctor within 1 week for follow-up</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">ACTIVITY:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Rest today and tomorrow</item>
              <item>Avoid strenuous exercise for 24-48 hours</item>
              <item>Benadryl may cause drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery</item>
              <item>Can return to normal activities once feeling completely better</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">QUESTIONS ANSWERED:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient verbalizes understanding of:</paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Need to avoid all shellfish permanently</item>
              <item>How and when to use EpiPen</item>
              <item>Importance of carrying two EpiPens at all times</item>
              <item>Warning signs requiring immediate return to ED</item>
              <item>Risk of biphasic reaction in next 12 hours</item>
              <item>Need for allergist follow-up</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Patient received written discharge instructions and demonstrates proper EpiPen technique.</content></paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Discharge time: 18:30</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Discharge condition: Stable, symptoms resolved</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Discharged to: Home with responsible adult</paragraph>

She was started on secondary prevention medications, including antiplatelet agents and statins. Discharge Medications: Aspirin 81 mg daily Atorvastatin 40 mg daily Clopidogrel 75 mg daily Lisinopril 10 mg daily for blood pressure control Follow-up: Follow up with neurologist in 2 weeks Continue physical and speech therapy outpatient Discharge Instructions: Take medications as prescribed Implement stroke prevention lifestyle changes (diet, exercise) Report any sudden neurological changes immediately

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">There is no current social history at the time of this document's creation.</text>

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph>32-year-old female presented to ED via EMS at 15:30 with acute onset allergic reaction. Patient reports eating shrimp scampi at local restaurant approximately 20 minutes prior to symptom onset. Within 5-10 minutes of eating, developed generalized urticaria, throat tightness, and difficulty breathing. Friend called 911 immediately.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>EMS arrived within 8 minutes and found patient in moderate distress with audible wheezing, tachypneic, diffuse hives, and facial angioedema. Patient was alert and oriented but anxious. EMS administered Epinephrine 0.3mg IM to right thigh at 15:20 with some improvement in breathing. IV access obtained en route. Patient transported to ED with lights and sirens, arrival at 15:30.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Patient denies prior history of shellfish allergy. Reports eating shrimp "many times before" without issue. Last ate shrimp approximately 6 months ago without problems. Denies recent illness, new medications, or other new exposures. Takes no daily medications. No history of asthma or prior anaphylaxis.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Review of systems: Denies chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. Endorses severe anxiety and feeling of "impending doom." Denies loss of consciousness or confusion.</paragraph>
          </text>
